Contract Title: DN2 Children?s Services Intervention Programme- Social Impact Bond
Department: Early Help and Children's Social Care
Contract Details: The DN2 Partnership - comprising Nottinghamshire County Council, Nottingham City Council, and Derby City Council - has been successful is securing funding to develop a Social Impact Bond.The Partnership is seeking to jointly commission evidence-based services that support children on the edge of care, and in care, to achieve better social outcomes. We believe that there is a strong evidence base demonstrating that children and young people achieve better outcomes when they are able to remain in a family environment. Through these services we collectively aim to support young people to achieve stable placements in the family setting, and where possible to remain at home with their families.We are seeking a Service Provider or consortia of Service Providers to deliver three distinct interventions across all three Local Authorities:Edge of Care - an intensive family intervention to support children and young people on the edge of care remain safely with their families. A young person on the edge of care is someone:? Who is not currently a Looked After Child (LAC) but who is identified as being at high risk of becoming LAC within the next 12 months; or? Who is currently a LAC who we are seeking to re-unify with their family.Placement Support - specialist provision to help young people who have entered residential care to step down to foster care, or specialist provision for those in foster care and at a high risk of entering residential care to remain in foster care.

Contract Title: TD1685 - Organic Waste Processing
Contract Details: Derby City Council (?the Council?) requires a service provider to provide Organic WasteBulking, Waste Transfer, Haulage and Processing Services from 9th of June 2021. TheCouncil is responsible for waste services for c.257,000 residents living in c.111,000households. The Council provides a service to residents which includes the fortnightlycollection of organic wastes (comingled garden waste and food waste) from 240l wheeledbins. It is anticipated that the Council will collect approximately 16 ktpa of Organic Waste atService Commencement. The Council anticipates that this volume will increase over theTerm, and could increase to c.22 ktpa. The organic waste will be tipped at a waste transferstation within the City and Bulked (Lot 1) and will then be hauled and delivered to anominated delivery point, via bulk haulage vehicles for processing and then processed (Lot2).

Contract Title: Therapeutic services framework for children adopted and placed on special guardianship order in the AEM region
Department: Early Help and Children's Social Care
Contract Details: Adoption East Midlands (AEM), is a Regional Adoption Agency hosted by Nottinghamshire County Council, delivering adoption services for 4 Local Authorities - Derbyshire County Council, Derby City Council, Nottinghamshire County Council and Nottingham City Council.Nottinghamshire County Council (the Council) on behalf AEM is seeking to establish a process to contract with organisations (Providers) able to provide Therapeutic Services (Framework). In addition, each individual LA?s within the AEM region, assumes responsibility for supporting children and families where a Special Guardianship Order is in place, therefore providers who join the Therapeutic Services Framework will also deliver the appropriate interventions to ensure positive outcomes for these families.Details of services that will be purchased under the Framework by AEM as set out in the service specification;The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals detailed below.Bidders may tender for one or more of the following Lots;?Lot 1: Play Therapies?Lot 2: Creative Therapies?Lot 3: Therapeutic/Attachment Focused Parenting Support & Programmes?Lot 4: Therapeutic Life Story work?Lot 5: Psychotherapy?Lot 6: Sensory Integration Processing Therapy?Lot 7: Group Programmes?Lot 8: Specialist AssessmentsFurther competitions will be limited to those Providers that successfully join the Framework, specific lots and Provider preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.DurationThis will be established with an initial duration of initial four-years, with option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.
CPV Code: 85000000-9 - Health and social work services, 85312000-9 - Social work services without accommodation

Contract Details: Adoption East Midlands (AEM), is a Regional Adoption Agency hosted by Nottinghamshire County Council, delivering adoption services for 4 Local Authorities - Derbyshire County Council, Derby City Council, Nottinghamshire County Council and Nottingham City Council.Nottinghamshire County Council (the Council) on behalf AEM is seeking to establish a process to contract with organisations (Providers) able to provide Therapeutic Services (Framework). In addition, each individual LA?s within the AEM region, assumes responsibility for supporting children and families where a Special Guardianship Order is in place, therefore providers who join the Therapeutic Services Framework will also deliver the appropriate interventions to ensure positive outcomes for these families.Details of services that will be purchased under the Framework by AEM as set out in the service specification;The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals detailed below.Bidders may tender for one or more of the following Lots;?Lot 1: Play Therapies?Lot 2: Creative Therapies?Lot 3: Therapeutic/Attachment Focused Parenting Support & Programmes?Lot 4: Therapeutic Life Story work?Lot 5: Psychotherapy?Lot 6: Sensory Integration Processing Therapy?Lot 7: Group Programmes?Lot 8: Specialist AssessmentsFurther competitions will be limited to those Providers that successfully join the Framework, specific lots and Provider preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.DurationThis will be established with an initial duration of initial four-years, with option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.
CPV Code: 85000000-9 - Health and social work services, 85312000-9 - Social work services without accommodation

These bids will be evaluated at set intervals detailed below.Bidders may tender for one or more of the following Lots;?Lot 1: Play Therapies?Lot 2: Creative Therapies?Lot 3: Therapeutic/Attachment Focused Parenting Support & Programmes?Lot 4: Therapeutic Life Story work?Lot 5: Psychotherapy?Lot 6: Sensory Integration Processing Therapy?Lot 7: Group Programmes?Lot 8: Specialist AssessmentsFurther competitions will be limited to those Providers that successfully join the Framework, specific lots and Provider preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.DurationThis will be established with an initial duration of initial four-years, with option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.
CPV Code: 85000000-9 - Health and social work services, 85312000-9 - Social work services without accommodation

Contract Title: D2N2 Children in Care Framework
Department: Early Help and Children's Social Care
Contract Details: Nottinghamshire County Council have, on behalf of Derbyshire County Council, Derby City Council, Nottingham City and Nottinghamshire County Council, (hence forth referred to as D2N2), completed the first phase to establish a hybrid Framework to provide independent Fostering and Residential Homes Placements provision, including where required out of D2N2 boundary placements. Services that will be purchased under the Framework by D2N2 will include individualised packages to meet the needs of the children and young people, and include Emergency, Planned, Short term, Long term, Permanent, Sibling, Solo, Respite, Parent and baby, and Remand Placements.  The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals, which will enable the D2N2 group to take advantage of emerging capacity whenever it is available and this should minimise the use of Spot contracting.Bidders are able to tender for one or more of the following Lots;Lot 1: Independent Residential Children?s Homes, that includes, Standard Children?s and Specialist Homes, Lot 2: Independent Fostering Agency Services, that includes, Standard Provision and Specialist Provision.In order to address some of the shortcomings of previous frameworks, Providers were asked to self-select if they are prepared to accept referrals for;i.Emergency Placementsii.Provision within the D2N2 foot printiii.Outside the D2N2 areaThis enables referrals to be limited to only those Providers by specific Lots and their own preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.The Framework has also been designed to enable the D2N2 group the ability to conduct other further competition processes , that could include Block arrangements, either collaboratively or as individual AuthoritiesThe Framework has been established with an initial duration of  four-years, with an option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.

Department: Early Help and Children's Social Care
Contract Details: Nottinghamshire County Council have, on behalf of Derbyshire County Council, Derby City Council, Nottingham City and Nottinghamshire County Council, (hence forth referred to as D2N2), completed the first phase to establish a hybrid Framework to provide independent Fostering and Residential Homes Placements provision, including where required out of D2N2 boundary placements. Services that will be purchased under the Framework by D2N2 will include individualised packages to meet the needs of the children and young people, and include Emergency, Planned, Short term, Long term, Permanent, Sibling, Solo, Respite, Parent and baby, and Remand Placements.  The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals, which will enable the D2N2 group to take advantage of emerging capacity whenever it is available and this should minimise the use of Spot contracting.Bidders are able to tender for one or more of the following Lots;Lot 1: Independent Residential Children?s Homes, that includes, Standard Children?s and Specialist Homes, Lot 2: Independent Fostering Agency Services, that includes, Standard Provision and Specialist Provision.In order to address some of the shortcomings of previous frameworks, Providers were asked to self-select if they are prepared to accept referrals for;i.Emergency Placementsii.Provision within the D2N2 foot printiii.Outside the D2N2 areaThis enables referrals to be limited to only those Providers by specific Lots and their own preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.The Framework has also been designed to enable the D2N2 group the ability to conduct other further competition processes , that could include Block arrangements, either collaboratively or as individual AuthoritiesThe Framework has been established with an initial duration of  four-years, with an option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.

The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals, which will enable the D2N2 group to take advantage of emerging capacity whenever it is available and this should minimise the use of Spot contracting.Bidders are able to tender for one or more of the following Lots;Lot 1: Independent Residential Children?s Homes, that includes, Standard Children?s and Specialist Homes, Lot 2: Independent Fostering Agency Services, that includes, Standard Provision and Specialist Provision.In order to address some of the shortcomings of previous frameworks, Providers were asked to self-select if they are prepared to accept referrals for;i.Emergency Placementsii.Provision within the D2N2 foot printiii.Outside the D2N2 areaThis enables referrals to be limited to only those Providers by specific Lots and their own preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.The Framework has also been designed to enable the D2N2 group the ability to conduct other further competition processes , that could include Block arrangements, either collaboratively or as individual AuthoritiesThe Framework has been established with an initial duration of  four-years, with an option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.

Department: Early Help and Children's Social Care
Contract Details: Nottinghamshire County Council have, on behalf of Derbyshire County Council, Derby City Council, Nottingham City and Nottinghamshire County Council, (hence forth referred to as D2N2), completed the first phase to establish a hybrid Framework to provide independent Fostering and Residential Homes Placements provision, including where required out of D2N2 boundary placements. Services that will be purchased under the Framework by D2N2 will include individualised packages to meet the needs of the children and young people, and include Emergency, Planned, Short term, Long term, Permanent, Sibling, Solo, Respite, Parent and baby, and Remand Placements.  The Framework will remain open for new tenders for the duration of its operation. These bids will be evaluated at set intervals, which will enable the D2N2 group to take advantage of emerging capacity whenever it is available and this should minimise the use of Spot contracting.Bidders are able to tender for one or more of the following Lots;Lot 1: Independent Residential Children?s Homes, that includes, Standard Children?s and Specialist Homes, Lot 2: Independent Fostering Agency Services, that includes, Standard Provision and Specialist Provision.In order to address some of the shortcomings of previous frameworks, Providers were asked to self-select if they are prepared to accept referrals for;i.Emergency Placementsii.Provision within the D2N2 foot printiii.Outside the D2N2 areaThis enables referrals to be limited to only those Providers by specific Lots and their own preference. The list of preferences will be refined over the life of the Framework.The Framework has also been designed to enable the D2N2 group the ability to conduct other further competition processes , that could include Block arrangements, either collaboratively or as individual AuthoritiesThe Framework has been established with an initial duration of  four-years, with an option to extend for a further two one-year extensions, to a maximum of six years.

Contract Title: TD1734 ? Derby City Public Health Local Health Services
Contract Details: Derby City Council is inviting Applications to establish a Preferred Provider List (PPL) for Public Health Local Health Services.These statutory demand led Services are to be delivered within the City of Derby and are split into the following Services and Lots:GP Services? Lot 1 - NHS Health Check Programme (approx �180,000GBP per annum)? Lot 2 - Provision, fitting, monitoring, management, and removal of Subcutaneous Contraceptive Implants (approx �110,000GBP per annum)? Lot 3 - Provision, fitting, management, and removal of Intra-uterine Contraception Device Fittings (IUCD) and Intra-uterine System (IUS) (approx �85,000GBP per annum)? Lot 4 - Primary Care Child and Family Weight Management Extended Brief Intervention Service(approx �60,000GBP per annum)Service Providers/Clinicians delivering the Service must be a registered member of the UK/Irish General Medical Council (GMC) or Royal College of Nursing (RCN) and have a license to practise, if required by your regulator.Pharmacy Services? Lot 5 - Pharmacy Based Needle Exchange (approx �60,000GBP per annum)? Lot 6 - Community Oral Emergency Contraception Services (approx �9,500GBP per annum) ? Service Provider must be Registered Health Professional in accordance with National PGD regulations? Lot 7 - Supervised Consumption (approx �355,000GBP per annum)Service Providers/Pharmacies delivering this Service must be registered with The General Pharmaceutical Council (GPhC).Applicants are must be able to meet the minimum requirements of the Specification(s).Bidders can bid for one or more Lots. Organisations with multiple Service delivery sites must complete an application per site (individual Applications). This means if you are a Group applying on behalf of various sites, that each GP Practice or Pharmacy site will require its own unique Login and Email Address for East Mids Tenders to submit separate individual Application(s). Applications solely on behalf of a Group will not be accepted.The initial Contract period is five years (5) the contract is estimated to commence 01 April 2022 and expire 31 March 2027.There will be an option to extend the Contract for two (2) years in annual increments, subject to satisfactory performance and business needs. Maximum Contract period is seven (7) years.The Total Value of this Contract is �6,016,500 over the maximum seven (7) year period.Please note - Service Providers wishing to express interest and obtain the documentation, must do so by 15:00 on 04 August 2021. It is recommended that you express interest and obtain the documentation well before this time to allow sufficient time to prepare and submit a comprehensive application. The deadline for Application Returns is 10:00 on 05 August 2021.Please visit the Council's e-tendering system https://www.eastmidstenders.org where you can express interest, obtain the documents and complete your application (Please note we will only accept Applications through the e-tendering system. Please search for TD1734.)Further Applicants may apply at anytime during the Contract Term.The ?Key Points Before You Start? document attached provides some brief tips.For help: Registration and Application guidance are provided in attached workflow and step by step documents attached.Full instructions and terms are provided Application (Process Overview) document available once you have registered your interest.

Contract Title: TD1955 - Derby Integrated Drug and Alcohol Recovery System
Contract Details:
Provision of the Derby Integrated Drug and Alcohol Treatment and Recovery System will discharge the following for Derby City Council:
?	Statutory service delivery in line with the conditions of Public Health Grant allocation in respect of substance misuse (section 31(4) of the Local Government Act 2003)
?	The duty to improve public health and address inequalities (steps considered appropriate for improving the health of the people in its area) (Health & Social Care Act 2012, s.12)
?	Deliver drug and alcohol treatment as a statutory requirement under the Crime and Disorder Act 1998 (un-repealed) in accordance with the Council?s Constitution.
?	Deliver a world-class treatment and recovery system as detailed in the national drug strategy From Harm to Hope: A 10-year drugs plan to cut crime and save lives (H.M. Government 2022) and the Dame Carol Black Independent Review of Drugs part two: prevention, treatment, and recovery.

In addition, the service will contribute to the substance misuse partnership priorities of:
?	Reducing drug and alcohol related deaths.

The Substance Misuse Young Person?s and Family support model will be embedded within the wider Children and Young Person?s offer for the city and is underpinned by robust partnership working and appropriate information sharing. A continuum of family focused interventions will be available to families affected by substance misuse which are strengths based, solution focused, trauma informed and empowering of recovery.

Contract Title: W22-098 - Project Assemble (Partnership Board) ? appointment of an independent Chair and project advisor
Contract Details:
The contract concerns the Council retaining the services of Mr. Graham Lister, an independent consultant who has detailed experience in the leadership and development of similar cultural projects to those proposed for the Assembly Rooms site. Mr. Lister?s expertise extends across the project cycle, including initial brokerage of complex partnerships between Local Authorities, Arts Council and local cultural bodies through to operational launch of a cultural building.

A tender waiver is being proposed due to Mr. Lister?s proven expertise in a highly specialised sector of early project development and project governance and his ability to boost the capacity of the partner organisations.  For this reason, Mr. Lister has been asked to work with the City Council, the University of Derby and Derby Theatre and to perform the role of an Independent Project Chair until the end of March 2024.

Value for Money is being achieved through direct management by the Council?s Project Manager as well as close interactions with the Project SRO (the Director of City Development Growth) and the Deputy Chief Executive (Communities and Place).

No alternatives to a waiver have been considered.  Mr. Lister has mentioned that he is on the 

If the waiver is not approved, the supplier will not be paid for work completed to date (c.�5,000) leading to the possibility of Mr. Lister seeking recourse from the Council for failing to honour a verbal contract.

The supplier has been selected due to his relevant experience in a highly specialised sub sector of management consultancy advice.  He is also known to Derby Theatre (and trusted as an ?honest broker?) through his work within the East Midlands (Leicester Curve) and as a former Board Director of DEDA.

The waiver sought is to cover a period to the end of March 2024. However, it should be noted that no resources are currently available to pay Mr. Lister beyond March 2023 and that unless they are identified by that date the current arrangement will cease.

Contract Title: TD1895 - Graffiti works at Moorways Sports Village
Contract Details:
Graffiti works at Moorways Sports Village.

Although the spend will be coded to Moorways Sports Village, funding has ultimately been approved from the Insurance Provision.  However, if requested, the service will seek to contribute to replenishing the insurance provision from the Moorways Sports Village revenue budget (4000870) as far as practical. 

The work is required to the external of the building cladding etc. at Moorways Sports Village, because of graffiti damage being applied to the external envelope.

Works:
1.	Clean the graffiti from the cladding etc. (revenue)
2.	Re-paint the cladding to an acceptable standard (revenue) 
3.	Add anti-graffiti coating (capital spend from main MSV Capital code and not included in this revenue spend figure) 

The total revenue costs are:

Works 	Cost 
Clean the cladding and re-paint the cladding (quote for 2 coats of paint)
	�48k
10% Contingency ? until the repaint is applied, it is unclear how many coats are required to bring the damaged are to the required standard, therefore a 10% contingency has been added to cover this 	10% of the total quote: 
�12k


The cost of the revenue works has benchmarked favourably against a quote obtained for similar works by the supplier.  The cost of paint, a key cost driver, has been sense checked by DCC officers.  Use of materials during the works will be monitored by Council Officers and an external Clerk of Works.

Note, in addition to cleaning, the costs of the anti-graffiti-coating is �70k. This is being funded from the MSV42 capital budget, C005316 Z0026, as approved by Project Team

The supplier is the only one approved by the cladding manufacturer, protecting the warranty on the panels.  They have experience of the site and the paint matching process.

Moorways Sports Village is a public building being used by thousands of people weekly. If the work to remove the graffiti is not undertaken, it will damage the reputation of the Council and the new operator. It also encourages others to graffiti the facility. 

The Capital funded anti-graffiti coating is being added following the Revenue funded works to remove the graffiti.  This will mean that, should the facility be graffitied in the future, it can be cleaned easily and at a much lower cost, by using a mild detergent.

Contract Title: TD1945 - Architectural and Design Services  (RIBA 3 TO 6) In respect of the site known as the Bowling Ground Rosehill Street (Butterfly project)
Contract Details:
The purpose of this procurement approval form is to cover an increase in architectural fees for a previously approved commission for the Rose Hills St (Butterfly Project) affordable housing scheme.

       

?	Approved commission 1 ? Heritage Impact assessment and design to RIBA 2 (�8,000 in total) 
?	Approved commission 2 ? RIBA 3-6 (�90,000 in total)  

A total of �23,000 has been spent so far. 

The architects fee proposal is based upon construction costs as follows:
Up to �2m ? 7%
Over �2m up to �4m ? 6.5%
Over �4m up to �5m ? 6%
�5m and over 5.5%

The initial architect fee proposal was based on 7% of construction costs (�1.5m) - �105,000 fee. However, under the current climate construction costs have rapidly increased due to increased costs of materials and labour, alongside this the site is a heritage site where we wish to deliver an exemplar scheme promoting lifetime homes, low carbon and biodiversity enhancements. Construction costs are now estimated at �2.6m (a 6.5% architectural fee applies) amounting to �169,000. Alongside the �64,000 increase, we wish to add a contingency of 10% (�16,900), which will also include the procurement of surveys via the Architect. Anticipated surveys to be:
Ecology Surveys including DEFRA Biodiversity Metric.

There could be a further increase in fee budget subject to an increase in build costs that would require our approval including an updated PAF approval. 

The development site is a heritage bowling green, anticipated to deliver 11 affordable homes of Passivhaus standard. The appointed architects (Anarchitecture Ltd T/A Conception) have the necessary heritage experience required to deal with the planning challenges on developing on a heritage site. They were appointed from the Councils Specialist Consultancy Services framework Lot 3 Heritage which expired 14 June 2019. The existing contract is in place up to RIBA 4 and can be extended up to RIBA 6, we are currently working on RIBA stages 2/3. We are looking at terminating the current contract as it is the Councils? standard T&Cs contract which provides inadequate liability and insurance cover for a construction project and moving over to councils non-novated consultant contract, which has been drafted by our advisers, Geldards. We are keen to keep Conception as the appointed architects due to their heritage experience, they have become familiar with the heritage site having already completed a reasonable amount of work including a feasibility design, heritage impact assessment and attending site and project meetings. 

It has been agreed with Finance colleagues that development projects within the HRA will normally have a capital budget code created once they secure Outline Business Case (Gateway 2) approval, however due to this being a heritage site which poses a big constraint to planning, a budget code will be created once planning approval has been granted (Gateway 3). Costs for this requirement are initially to be paid from Capital pre- Development cost code C005810 Z0031 and then recharged to individual projects? capital budgets as and when they are created within 23/24 FY.

The development will be designed to meet the needs of the local area (Normanton) where there is a significant shortage of suitably adapted homes, properties that are energy efficient and a lack of resident parking. The ultimate benefits will be the provision of around 11 affordable homes to Derby residents, with the benefits to health and wellbeing as described in the Council?s Housing Strategy. The provision of these homes will in turn reduce the numbers of people on the social housing waiting list and increase Council Income long term.

Contract Title: TD1948 -  TCF Strategic Corridors Supply, Installation & Maintenance of Electronic Passenger Information Display screens at Bus Stops
Contract Details:
Derby have been awarded �61m as part of the Transforming Cities Fund, which is to fund a number of projects across Derby, including public transport, walking and cycling schemes.  The end date for spending this funding is provisionally 31 March 2024, this is awaiting confirmation from the Department for Transport.  This programme is a joint scheme with Nottingham City Council and was approved by Cabinet on 10 June 2020 to be added to the work programme.

The aim of the project is to improve the connectivity of all journeys across Derby and provide transport choices to reduce the dependency on the car and improve the associated infrastructure to ensure that the user has the best experience.

As part of the strategic corridor project, 111 electronic information displays will be provided at key bus stops on the arterial routes.  These signs provide confidence and assurance to the passengers waiting for the bus service and can reduce the need for paper timetable information at the bus stops.  

Within the cost of the proposal is a 5 warranty, maintenance, and support package to ensure the smooth delivery of the works and to manage the ongoing system and upgrade as necessary.

The supply and installation of the displays is being procured through an existing framework with the D2N2 partners, providing value for money.

Contract Title: TD1902 - Ecological Advice
Contract Details:
The Council requires ecological advice with respect to Local Wildlife Sites subject to planning applications, Local Plan allocations or other development proposals. This includes:
?	the assessment and analysis of potential impacts and advice and/or recommendations as to possible alternatives or mitigation.
?	work associated with Local Plan Examinations and planning inquiries, i.e. the preparation of supporting evidence, meetings and the appearance at the examination or inquiry if required or the provision of written evidence if required.
?	the assessment of all biodiversity metrics submitted by applicants prior to Biodiversity Net Gain becoming mandatory in November 2023.

The Council also requires the maintenance of a robust, high quality Local Wildlife Sites register for the Councils area using the existing Wildlife Sites System (the Derbyshire Biological Records Centre) used county-wide 
The Council intends to enter into an agreement with Derbyshire Wildlife Trust Limited (DWTL) for ongoing ecological advice and the maintenance of the existing Wildlife Sites System (the Derbyshire Biological Records Centre) used county-wide.
The negotiated procedure without prior publication is being used and is justified under Regulation 32(2)(b) (iii) of the Public
Contracts Regulations 2015 for the award of a public services contract. 
The services can only be carried out by the particular economic operator for the following reasons:
(iii) the protection of exclusive rights, including intellectual property rights.
Competition is absent as the intellectual property   in the Derbyshire Biological Records Centre is owned by DWTL.

Contract Title: TD1910 - Highways Resource Support Framework
Contract Details:
Highways Maintenance requires a resource support contract in order to provide additional resource to our Maintenance service, this is so we are able to deliver both our statutory duty to maintain the highway under the highway act 1980 but also mainly for income generating works and projects 

The roles required are as follows: 

-	Semi-Skilled Roadworks with appropriate experience and qualifications 
-	Skilled Roadworks with appropriate experience and qualifications 
-	Two-man crew to include semi-skilled & skilled roadworkers with appropriate experience and qualifications 
-	Four-man crew to include two semi-skilled & two skilled roadworkers with appropriate experience and qualifications
-	JCB including Operator 
-	7.5 Ton vehicle 
-	3.5 Ton Vehicle 
-	Small plant (saw,wacker,breakers)

Highways will require the above in line with the following timescales: 

Semi- skilled and Skilled Roadworker ? within 24 of request 
Two man crew ? within 3 days 
Four man crew ? within 5 days 

Additional resource support is required to enable the service to meet the demands of existing and new clients and the short-term approach to work/project/scheme programming as well as enabling the service to be more flexible in its delivery of reactive, emergency, and statutory works.

The risks to the service and ultimately the council of not approving this request would be a loss of income, resulting in a loss of income which would have a negative effect on the highway budget, and the inability of the service to be flexible in its delivery of reactive, income generating, emergency, and statutory duty.

Contract Title: ENQ826 - Commissioning a Joint Local Housing Needs Assessment for Derby and South Derbyshire
Contract Details:
Council Cabinet approved commencement of work on a new Local Plan in December 2021. National Planning policy requires that Local Plans are underpinned by relevant, robust and up to date evidence. This is reflected in Planning Practice Guidance (PPG) which requires Local Planning Authorities to prepare a Local Housing Needs Assessment (LHNA) to understand the housing needs that the Council needs to plan for.  

The purpose of the LHNA is to identify local housing needs to 2039; in particular to identify the needs for specialist housing, including Affordable Housing, homes required for older people and people with disabilities, the sizes of homes required and land required for people to build their own homes. The commission will also require consultants to consider need and demand for Housing in Multiple Occupation (HMO) in Derby, the need for further student accommodation and evidence to further understand the role and need for Supported Exempt Accommodation (SEA) in the City.  

The last time a similar study was undertaken was in 2013, to underpin the adopted Local Plan.  

The outcomes of the study will be used to inform Local Plan polices, including affordable housing requirements and housing site allocations. The assessment will also evidence the need for, and extent of, potential Article 4 directions related to HMO accommodation and to inform joint working on cross boundary matters with neighbouring local authorities. The newly formed SEA team will also use the evidence to inform and improve SEA provision in the City. 

There is no expertise in-house to take on this wide ranging assessment and as such we are seeking to appoint external consultants (Iceni Projects Ltd) via a direct award from the ESPO framework. Iceni Projects Ltd are recognised specialists in this field and have undertaken similar work for other neighbouring authorities. The staff involved in the consultancy were also behind the last housing needs work undertaken for the City in 2013. 

To reduce costs and reflect the broad areas of work being commissioned, the study is a joint commission with between the Planning Policy and Supported Exempt Accommodation Teams at the City Council along with the Planning Policy Team at South Derbyshire District Council (SDDC). The Planning Policy Team at Derby City Council are leading on the procurement of consultants. A fee of �44,458 has been quoted, which will be split between the authorities, providing economies of scale. This cost split in set out in the table in the attached document, with the City Council spend amounting to �24,715. South Derbyshire District council will be invoiced separately and therefore spending panel approval for the City elements of this commission will be sought on completion of this PAF.

Contract Title: TD1863 - Derby Car Club Operator
Contract Details:
As part of the Future Transport Zone (FTZ) side of the programme there is an urgent requirement to procure, implement and support a city-wide car club in the City.  This is an essential element of key projects, including Mobility as a Service (MaaS) platforms, EV charging infrastructure and e-Mobility hubs in communities, campuses and depots.  

Car clubs provide low emission mobility which helps to break dependency on private car ownership. Pay as you go cars offer affordable, occasional access to cars to benefit individuals. At the same time, they help policy makers to meet targets at local, regional and national levels, including emissions reduction, improvements to air quality and encouraging individuals to increase their use of sustainable modes.

The proposal is for the Council to tender for a Concessionaire to establish and operate a fully electric car club in the city of Derby, with a phased approach that will start early 2023 (subject to resources and guidance from procurement and legal).  The concession will utilise existing bays left over from the previous scheme, new bays being implemented via FTZ programme (such as those in Council owned car park), installations as part of new developments and community-based car clubs.  We intend to install a minimum of 15 operational bays within the first 3 years of the contract, with no upper limited on the number of vehicles to be included in the car club offer, and the Concessionaire shall work with the City Council to grow and develop the network.

Funding for the EV charging infrastructure is available under the existing D2N2 framework agreement, although further discussions on how this will work under the new contract need to be agreed with Nottingham City Council and BP Pulse. Other non-contractual costs including TRO development, project management and Signing and Lining, will be covered by FTZ funding, but they do not form part of the contract with the Operator.
Supplier Name: ENTERPRISE RENT-A-CAR UK LIMITED T/A ENTERPRISE CAR CLUB

Contract Title: TD1994 - Require Consultancy Services in the delivery of new council homes at the Bateman Street Site (Former site Ivy House School), under a future Design and Build Contract.
Contract Details:
July 23 - Architects no longer to sub-consult Engineering Services'

Appointment of HLP (Halsall Lloyd Partnership) through Procurement Partnerships Professional Services Framework, a fully PCR 2015 compliant framework notified on Find a Tender Service (formerly the OJEU), to provide the following services in the delivery of new council homes at the Bateman Street Site (Former site Ivy House School), under a future Design and Build Contract.  
?	Architectural and Landscape Architect Services
?	Principal Designer
?	Civil and Structural Engineers, including Flood Risk Assessment, Sustainable Drainage System, Contaminated Ground Reports, S38/S278 Technical approval, S104 Surface and Foul tech approval
?	Topographical survey
?	Underground Utility Mapping Survey
?	Preliminary Ecology Appraisal Survey and Biodiversity Net Gain calculation
?	Flood Risk Assessment Report
?	Acoustic Assessment

The development site is a disused site, which was formerly the location of Ivy House School off Bateman Street, which was demolished many years ago. It is anticipated that the site will deliver 20 affordable energy efficient homes with associated landscaping and parking facilities. The properties will be a mixture of 2, 3 and 4 bedroom houses to meet the demand on the social housing waiting list.

When reviewing the Architectural Consultants on the Procurement Partnerships Professional Services Framework, there were only three who evidenced affordable housing experience, of these three HLP were the most competitively priced, had the highest quality score and were locally based in Nottingham.
HLP will sub-consult the Structural and Civil Engineers and procure the additional surveys and reports. We have reviewed the detailed scope of services procured under the Procure Partnerships Professional Services Framework for the Architectural services and they are satisfactory for the Council?s needs, and we therefore propose to appoint using a Direct Award. 

The additional services of Engineering and Surveys/ Reports are outside of the Procurement Partnerships Framework, but we have been advised that we can appoint HLP for these services under the same Direct Award. HLP have provided three quotes for each additional service and reviewed the scopes of services, so quotes are competitive and fulfil the Projects requirements. 

I have used an estimated construction cost of �4.13m. This is a precautionary approach given the current extraordinary volatility of the market for both materials and labour, and the resultant difficulty in forecasting, as well as the Council?s requirements for new homes to include misting systems and to meet the Future Homes Standard of thermal efficiency

The fees for each service excluding VAT are as follows: 
?	1.49% of construction cost for Architectural and Landscape Architect Services �61,537
?	0.18% of construction cost for Principal Designer �7,434
?	Civil and Structural Engineers, including Flood Risk Assessment, Sustainable Drainage System, Contaminated Ground Reports, S38/S278 Technical approval, S104 Surface and Foul tech approval - �28,110.00
?	Topographical survey �675.00
?	Underground Utility Mapping Survey �1,350.00
?	Preliminary Ecology Appraisal and BNG - �1,500.00
?	Acoustic Assessment �1,395.50

This totals, �102,001.50, to which we have added a 10% contingency - �10,200.12

As final total construction cost cannot be known at this early stage, there could be an increase in fee budget subject to an increase in build cost, that would require client approval and an updated PAF would need to be approved. Any increases to the budget will require client approval through the change control procedures set out by Change Derby.

It has been agreed with Finance colleagues that development projects within the HRA will normally have a capital budget code created once planning approval has been granted (Gateway 3). Costs for this requirement are initially to be paid from Capital pre- Development cost code 685810 and then recharged to individual projects? capital budgets as and when they are created within 23/24 FY.

The ultimate benefits will be the provision of around 20 affordable homes to Derby residents, with the benefits to health and wellbeing as described in the Council?s Housing Strategy. The provision of these homes will in turn reduce the number of people on the social housing waiting list and increase Council Income in the long term.

Contract Title: ENQ819 - Research to assess the feasibility of provision of low/zero interest loans
Contract Details:
Aims include: 

?	That a workable programme is identified that would enable households eligible for mandatory Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) to self-fund costs for adaptations that are above the maximum grant
?	For such a programme to also enable homeowners and residential landlords to fund or part fund retrofit carbon reduction measures to their homes
?	To enable homeowners to: 
o	Self-fund costs that are above the level of grant for which they are eligible
o	Supplement grants for which they are eligible
o	Self-fund adaptations or energy efficiency improvements to their home for which no grants are available. 

To also consider if such a model could also be used for other similar requirements. 

Outcomes would be expected to include:
?	An overview of current arrangements and obstacles to accessing funding for the purposes described
?	A summary of discussions / interviews with relevant operational services areas in the local authorities concerned to ensure a clear analysis of the current situation and pertinent issues
?	A full Final Report including an options appraisal 
?	Up to 4 presentations on the research findings to interested parties (at least one to be ?in person? in the Derby/Nottingham area, if required)

Contract Title: TD1827 - eLearning System
Contract Details:
Our current E learning contract with our suppliers RSM and Learning Nexus is due to finish on the 
31
st December 2021 and originally we were looking to go back out to tender to assess the current 
market and follow the procurement process for a new contract with a supplier. Due to the impact 
that COVID 19 has had on the organisation and also given the financial position it was agreed that 
this was not the right time to do this with a contract that is critical to the learning and development 
of the whole workforce. It was agreed with procurement that we could continue with our current 
suppliers and existing contact with a view to extend up to 4 years but with the option of reviewing 
on a yearly basis. The cost associated will remain the same for 2 years under the contact 
agreement and will be reviewed in year 3 if there are any changes proposed. 
On checking the current e learning supplier market this price is the lowest that can be offered for 
our requirements. 
We are currently in the process of discussing with our e learning supplier about system upgrades 
that they have made that we can incorporate into our contract at no extra cost to help us make 
several improvements that are necessary. 
The main IT involved elements include single sign on for users allowing for an easier and more 
secure way of accessing the e learning system for users. Also to allow a secure data transfer file 
from our hr system Itrent to our e learning supplier for at least monthly data updates. This includes 
adding new starters, removal of leavers, transfer and hierarchy updates. 
This will allow for more accurate and regular data updates and ensure less room for errors or 
inaccurate data as currently this process is done manually. 
Preferably users will be able to access the e learning system via mobile devices (eg mobile phone 
and tablet) as well as the standard online access (eg laptop and computer). 
The system upgrades will also allow for a range of updates that will really assist our managers and 
users in their learning and development as well as cut down a lot of manual admin work from our 
side each month. 
? Managers will have their own dashboards so that they can track their employees learning 
progress and pull their own reports which at present cannot be done by anyone other than 
the admin post within OD team 
? Employees will be able to reset their own passwords, which is currently only able to be 
done by an admin role in the OD team 
? Ability to create more content in house instead of paying other suppliers to design and 
create e learning content for us so that we can install it onto our internal system
? Allow trainers to populate training courses onto the system so that employees can book 
onto training courses, add to waiting lists, complete training through the system and then 
complete evaluation forms via the system so that it is all in one central system.
Supplier Name: RSM UK Risk Assurance Services LLP

Contract Title: ENQ793 - Vending Machine Provider
Contract Details:
Springwood Leisure Centre requires a vending service to provide refreshment and re-fuelling for its gym members. The vending revenue will also provide an important revenue and profit stream for the service area.

Stores Road requires a vending service to provide a subsidised hot drinks service to its employees. This agreement has been brought about by union negotiation. Stores Road employees are working outdoors in all weather conditions and need access to a provision of hot drinks. Snacks and cold drinks are also available however these are not subsidised.

The previous supply agreement was with GEM Vending who were subsequently sold to Selecta.

Selecta have provided a very poor service into Springwood and Stores Road. The leisure centre had its machines withdrawn without notice and Stores Road have received no service for repairs to damaged machines.

In September 2020, Selecta wrote to Derby City Council advising that they were withdrawing service due to staff shortages/issues around the impact of the pandemic. We are currently out of contract to our vending supplier and see this as an opportunity to review the contract with a view to achieving better value for money and better service.

Springwood Leisure Centre will lease new machines from a newly sourced provider on a self-managed contract together with a service agreement that will see the machines maintained over the life of the agreement. The leisure centre staff will purchase stock and fill the machines themselves with cash revenues being reconciled on site and banked within the service area. Card payments will be paid directly to the service area via a third party- Nayax.

Stores Road will operate with a fully managed service where all profits from the sales of snacks and cold drinks will be retained by the vending provider. Hot drinks will be subsidised at 20p and this will be paid for from the profits of hot drinks/rental of the machines. There will be a service agreement in place that will see the machines maintained over the life of the rental agreement. Card payments would be made direct to the vending provider via a third party Nayax.

This service is essential to guarantee the completion of the OCOR Munio project environmental and biodiversity commitments and the successful delivery of the Mill Fleam project construction by 2023.

Servicing and maintenance costs for these are currently increasing as reliability is becoming an issue. These items need to be replaced as they are now deemed to be life expired. We also wish to order a further 5 vehicles which are due for replacement in FY 2024/2025. It has been agreed that capital will be released early so we can order these vehicles before the end of FY 2022/2023 otherwise we will be exposed to a 10.1% price increase from Terberg Matec which will be effective as of 01/04/2023. Ordering these earlier will ensure we are not exposed to the price increase which will save The Council approximately �295K.

It is anticipated that an existing Nottingham Consortium Framework Agreement for the purchase of refuse collection vehicles will be used to tap into a ready supply of such vehicles from Terberg Matec. This eliminates significant amounts of work and time in the tendering process.
It is expected that these vehicles will have a life expectancy of 10 years.

Contract Title: TD1892 - Washroom Services
Contract Details:
Provide hygiene & sanitary services to various Council wide sites including, Parks, Rycote Centre, Kedleston Road Centre, libraries, Derby Live and Council House. 
Goods and services should meet the quality standards expected by Derby City Council. The Workplace (Health, Safety and Welfare) Regulation 1992 specifies that all companies are required to provide a suitable means for the disposal of sanitary dressings in ladies washrooms. Furthermore, The Environmental Protection Act 1990 Section 34 and Duty of Care Regulations 1991, also known as the Duty of Care Act, demands that all sanitary waste is managed to the point of disposal. Waste must be carried by a licensed carrier with a valid carrier?s certificate and a full audit trail of documentation must be always available. Failure to comply can lead to a large fine or imprisonment. 
To not approve the contract would have detrimental effects on employees, customers, and visitors. 
This contract will have the ability to offer efficiencies and consistency through a procurement route.
